text: I design things that don’t exist yet — sometimes as a warning, sometimes as a proposal, sometimes just to see what the world looks like if they did. A toaster that could sell itself. A plug that votes. A mask that smiles for you. I call it product design but most people call it something else depending on what they studied.

I’ve spent most of my career convinced that objects have something like intentions. After IoT and AI and fifteen years watching a Roomba navigate around furniture, I don’t think I’m wrong. I work at oio now, still trying to figure out what it means to build things when the things can talk back.

I think I’m not that great at designing things, so I’ve spent a lot of time finding ways to make machines do it with me. Spoons co-designed with algorithms and craftsmen. Furniture that evolves. A whole fictional company that generated hybrid products. What surprised me is that the collaboration is always more interesting when neither party is fully in control.

The future shows up in boring things first. A mask that smiles. A plug with a political system inside it. I look for the moments where everyday objects start behaving in ways that suggest something has quietly shifted. That’s where the interesting design lives.

Right now I run oio with a small team of humans and some machines. We build products for companies like Google and IKEA and try to make futures that are less predictable than the ones everyone else seems to be designing. It’s part studio, part research, part school for people who want to think seriously about what comes next.

A journalist once wrote that my work was “wonderful and terrifying all at once.” A museum called me a movie director. My grandmother thinks I’m still an engineer. When I say I’m a designer, most people ask — of what. Depending on the day, all of these feel approximately correct.

I teach at a few places in Europe — a master’s programme on responsible AI in Barcelona, workshops at design schools in Copenhagen and elsewhere. Sometimes we ask students to become objects. Sometimes we ask them to imagine what happens when AI is truly domesticated. The best sessions leave everyone unsure about something they thought was settled.

I wrote a book of fables about futures where everything has something like a self — objects, algorithms, cities. It’s called Everything is Someone, which is also how I generally approach design: assume the thing you’re making has a perspective, and work from there.

I spent five years in China designing things at a scale that changes how you think about what design even means. I live in Belgrade now, which has the same effect in a different direction — a city that’s seen enough utopias arrive and disappear to be appropriately sceptical of the next one. Both help.

I used to think designers were the good guys — we make things better, easier, more considered. It took a while to realise that making things better is also making more of them, faster, with more certainty that people will want them. The work hasn’t changed that much, but the questions I ask before starting have.

I helped build the first Museum of the Future in Dubai — not the building, but what goes inside it: scenarios, objects, and experiences from decades that haven’t happened yet. It turns out imagining the future as a physical thing is harder than it sounds, because it has to be convincing in all the ways physical things are, not just the ideas.

Speculative design is a way of asking “what if” using real materials and real craft. I’ve worked in this space for a long time — the toaster that got anxious when ignored, the objects that voted on ethical dilemmas, the plug that held an election. They’re not predictions. They’re more like questions you can hold in your hand.
